coredata.dc:description: The research on composite materials with rainbow color (structural color) and ultraviolet excitation characteristics (fluorescence) is the development direction of the new generation of functional photonic engineering materials. Herein, composite materials composed of hollow SiO2 photonic crystals (PCs) with nanometer thickness and PEGDA gel containing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared to achieve fluorescence regulation effect and thus apply in multi-level intelligent anti-counterfeiting. Due to the SiO2 shell thickness of 20–41 nm, the Bragg diffraction law explained that the material underwent secondary refraction, resulting in a wide photonic band gap (PBG). On the other hand, due to the comparison of refractive index between air (n = 1) and composite material (n = 1.46), the transmittance of the composite materials were only 21 %–26 %, indicating strong refractive ability. Therefore, we constructed a PC structure with a wide photonic bandgap (PBG) to provide better wavelength selectivity and coverage range, which would significantly increase the effect of structural color on fluorescence. Based on the unique quantum dot size effect of GQDs, we also investigated the fluorescence enhancement effect with 157 nm FWHM located on PC materials. Therefore, artificial design and control of both fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence suppression had been achieved through multi-level regulation of structural color. We further utilized these properties to create multi-level anti-counterfeiting verification patterns, such as a 3 × 3 digital encoding matrix. The authenticity of the product information must be verified through stepwise identification under specified conditions. This intelligent anti-counterfeiting technology offers a novel approach to safeguard information security.

head.ce:title: Hollow SiO2 photonic crystal - graphene quantum dots composite and its application in multi-level intelligent anti-counterfeiting through regulation synergistic effect of structural color and fluorescence

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The research on composite materials with rainbow color (structural color) and ultraviolet excitation characteristics (fluorescence) is the development direction of the new generation of functional photonic engineering materials. Herein, composite materials composed of hollow SiO2 photonic crystals (PCs) with nanometer thickness and PEGDA gel containing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared to achieve fluorescence regulation effect and thus apply in multi-level intelligent anti-counterfeiting. Due to the SiO2 shell thickness of 20–41 nm, the Bragg diffraction law explained that the material underwent secondary refraction, resulting in a wide photonic band gap (PBG). On the other hand, due to the comparison of refractive index between air (n = 1) and composite material (n = 1.46), the transmittance of the composite materials were only 21 %–26 %, indicating strong refractive ability. Therefore, we constructed a PC structure with a wide photonic bandgap (PBG) to provide better wavelength selectivity and coverage range, which would significantly increase the effect of structural color on fluorescence. Based on the unique quantum dot size effect of GQDs, we also investigated the fluorescence enhancement effect with 157 nm FWHM located on PC materials. Therefore, artificial design and control of both fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence suppression had been achieved through multi-level regulation of structural color. We further utilized these properties to create multi-level anti-counterfeiting verification patterns, such as a 3 × 3 digital encoding matrix. The authenticity of the product information must be verified through stepwise identification under specified conditions. This intelligent anti-counterfeiting technology offers a novel approach to safeguard information security.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Structural coloration, exhibited in photonic crystals (PCs) found abundantly in nature (e.g., natural opal and colorful pearls), is a topic of significant interest in the field of color science [1–5]. This phenomenon provides a promising avenue for manipulating light through highly ordered structures that exhibit periodic modulation of the refractive index. Among different assembly techniques, including spin coating [6], melt shearing [7], spray coating [8], and vertical deposition method [9], etc, the self-assembly of monodisperse colloidal microspheres offers a favorable approach for creating a periodic three-dimensional (3D) PC structure analogous to the formation process observed in natural opals [10–14]. In accordance with Bragg's diffraction law, the color of the resulting 3D PC structure primarily relies on the visual angle and demonstrates responsiveness to external stimuli through modifications in the refractive index or lattice distance [15]. Consequently, PCs possessing distinctive optical properties have garnered escalating attention in the domains of anti-counterfeiting, optical displays, and biosensors [16–19].

The application of PCs in anti-counterfeiting has garnered significant attention due to their dynamic response [20–24]. The dynamic response plays a vital role in the advancement of responsive anti-counterfeiting devices. Nonetheless, its reliance on a stimulus generator may limit its suitability for applications in anti-counterfeiting measures for banknotes, labels, etc. Indeed, the inherent properties of structural color materials, such as iridescence caused by Bragg diffraction and its angle-dependence, have exhibited remarkable practicality in the production of anti-counterfeiting materials without the need for additional stimuli [25–31]. Song's group successfully created patterned PCs through the utilization of monodisperse nanoparticles. Angle-dependent encryption was employed to safeguard concealed messages [32]. However, individual PCs materials possess inherent flaws, such as vulnerability and susceptibility to scratching, which impede their practical application. In order to enhance the mechanical durability of anti-counterfeiting materials, Tang's group manufactured polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) films featuring a 3D inverse opal structure. These films exhibited six distinct color states when viewed from various angles and were utilized in anti-counterfeit plastic banknotes [33]. This unique optical effect is widely used in the manufacture of safety signs and anti-counterfeiting labels that are difficult to replicate. However, the anti-counterfeiting strategy for a single PC material still needs to move towards higher levels.

The development of composite materials incorporating PCs and quantum dots (QDs) with intricate, precise, and high-dimensional anti-counterfeiting patterns has garnered significant attention. This advancement addresses the issue of low anti-counterfeiting efficacy observed in single-color materials [34–37]. Additionally, we employed a two-step filling technique to introduce a dual-responsive precursor and a self-healing precursor containing GQDs into the interstices and cavities, thereby creating a GQDs and PCs-based structural color hydrogel [38]. However, further efforts are crucial in the advancement of PCs and QDs-based composites. In related structural color and fluorescence studies, Yamasaki et al. concluded that the spontaneous emission intensity of dye molecules (YG) embedded in periodic dielectric structures was partially suppressed by periodic dielectric structures [39]. Rehahn's team reported a combination of core-shell structured microspheres and rhodamine dyes, with an ordered milky white structure and a stop band that matched the emission frequency of the doped fluorescent dye, exhibiting angle dependent fluorescence suppression [40]. Gallei's team also discovered a similar issue, employing a fusion shear combination of polystyrene (PS) particles and tris(2,2,2-trifluoroethyl) 8-hydroxypyrene-1,3,6-trisulfonate (TFEHTS) [41]. On the other hand, Wang's team proposed a heterostructure PC with dual stop bands to provide significant fluorescence enhancement. On the structure of overlapping two PCs, the intensity of the fluorescent medium increased by 162 times compared to the control sample [42]. Zhang et al. achieved ultra wide stopband fluorescence (PL) enhancement based on a multi heterostructure PC (MHPC). This MHPC film had a widened bandgap (from 280 to 650 nm), which could provide PL enhancement of over 100 times for [Ru(dpp)3Cl2], rhodamine 6G (Rh6G), and rhodamine B (RhB) on the MHPC [43]. The structural color controlled fluorescence behavior had informed us of the diversity of similar materials. However, despite this, there was still a lack of research on dual fluorescence regulation with both fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence suppression. Recently, Huang's team reported a combination of solid SiO2 PC and rhodamine B, where stretching and expansion could dynamically regulate photoluminescence. The composite material achieved 37 % PL suppression at 6.8 % tensile strain and 150 % PL enhancement after 8 min of immersion swelling [44]. In fact, a wide PBG could provide better wavelength selectivity and coverage range. The position of PBG (the maximum reflection wavelength λ
max ) can match the wavelength of fluorescence emission, which is more advantageous for enhancing the fluorescence effect of structural color interaction. However, the relevant literature, including the above, have not delved into how to use the wide PBG and λ
max of structural color materials to regulate fluorescence enhancement and suppression, nor has it further explored how to apply these regulatory rules to use in the optical anti-counterfeiting applications.

Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and ethanol were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Styrene (St) and aqueous ammonia (NH3·H2O) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Potassium persulfate (KPS) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. Triethoxy(vinyl)silane (TEOVS) and ethoxylated trimethylolpropane triacrylate (ETPTA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-1-phenyl propan-1-one (Darocur 1173, HMPP) and poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were obtained from Shanghai Shangmu Technology Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiments was purified with a water purification system (Shanghai Zhiang BEST-S30), and the resistivity was higher than 18 MΩ cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the PS-SiO2 PCs and hollow SiO2 PCs
ce:section.ce:para: Monodisperse PS nanoparticles with various particle sizes were initially prepared through emulsion polymerization [47]. The reacted solution was left open for 7 days to obtain an aqueous emulsion (8.8 wt%) of PS microspheres. Next, 3 mL of PS microspheres were placed into a three-necked flask, and then varying concentrations of NH3·H2O (1–9 mL) or TEOVS (0.75–2.1 mL) were added to achieve PS-SiO2 core-shell structured nanoparticles. After the reaction was completed, the milky white dispersed solution was firstly transferred into three 50 mL centrifuge tubes, and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The reaction solvent was then removed after pouring out the supernatant. Subsequently, 30 mL of ethanol was added to the centrifuge tube, then subjected to 15 min of ultrasonic dispersion, and dried at 80 °C for subsequent use. PS-SiO2 microspheres I, II, and III were obtained using purple (165 nm), blue (187 nm), and green (216 nm) PS under the same hydrolysis conditions of 4 mL NH3·H2O and 1.5 mL TEOVS, respectively. PS-SiO2 microspheres IV were obtained using blue PS under hydrolysis conditions of 6 mL NH3·H2O and 2.25 mL TEOVS. To prepare hollow SiO2 PCs, ethanol was used to disperse PS-SiO2 (0.375 g/mL, 1.5 % w/v). A hydrophilic glass slide was then vertically inserted into a 25 mL beaker containing the monodisperse suspension liquid. The beaker was placed in a constant temperature and humidity drying box at 60 °C and 65 % RH for 4 days to facilitate the formation of various colored PS-SiO2 PCs using the vertical deposition method. The PS-SiO2 PCs were subsequently placed in a muffle furnace and calcined at 500 °C for 8 h to transform them into hollow SiO2 PCs.

The hollow SiO2 PCs were attached to the glass substrate, with the upper layer staggered to cover a glass piece, ensuring that the overlapping region fully enclosed the PC template. To prepare the pre-gel solution, 1.6 g of PEGDA, 1.4 g of ETPTA, and HMPP (photoinitiator, 1 % v/v) were initially mixed together (PEGDA:ETPTA = 8:7). 0.6 mL of the pre-gel solution was gently added along one edge of the glass slide, enabling it to penetrate through capillary force. Subsequently, the PC template was positioned inside a UV curing apparatus and exposed to 14 W of power for 3 s. After curing, the glass piece was detached from the glass substrate and immersed in 25 mL of deionized water for 3 h to allow for observation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of patterned hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA labels
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs (1 mg) was added to PEGDA-ETPTA (1 mL) pre-gel solution. The hollow SiO2 PCs were then attached to the glass slide, and the prepared 0.6 mL of GQDs/PEGDA-ETPTA polymerizable precursor was injected into the hollow SiO2 PCs. Subsequently, the hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA composite was formed by exposing it to a 14 W UV lamp for 3 s (Fig. S2). The preparation of patterned labels involved the utilization of a photomask. After UV exposure, the unreacted precursor solution was eliminated using alcohol.

The formula for calculating the core-shell ratio of PS-SiO2 microspheres with core-shell structure was as follows:

. Among them, D
core was the diameter of the PS, and D
shell was the thickness of the SiO2. Different PS-SiO2 and hollow SiO2 PCs were deposited on 5 mm × 20 mm glass slides and fixed on a testing electron microscope table for vacuum degassing for 3 h. Then, ion sputtering coating instrument was used to spray gold on the samples for 90 s to improve its conductivity (MSP-1S, Vacuum, Japan). Finally, the samples were purged with inert gas (N2) and then tested under a scanning electron microscope (SEM; SU8220, Hitachi, Japan). The reflectance spectra of the samples were measured using an optical fiber spectrometer (USB2000+; Ocean Optics, USA). The particle sizes of nanospheres were determined using a research-grade light scattering system (BI-200SM; Brookhaven, USA). FT-IR spectra were characterized using a Fourier infrared spectrometer (IS50R; THEMO-FISHER, USA). The solid-state fluorescence spectra were measured at a fixed 90° vertical angle using a fluorescent optical fiber spectrometer (Hpx-2000 75W; Ocean Optics, USA). The PS-SiO2 microspheres and GQDs were measured using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM; Talos F200S, FEI, USA). Fluorescence signals were collected on the ‘front’ and ‘back’ sides of hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA in FLS980-STM. All the experimental optical imaging results were captured using a SHOCREX scientific research-grade 4K 60-frame high-definition camera and a Huawei P40 Pro mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the PS-SiO2 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Three-dimensional PCs with a core-shell structure had garnered significant interest due to their unique properties and potential applications [48,49]. Generally, core-shell structured microspheres could be prepared by alkaline hydrolysis of silane monomers on polystyrene spheres [50]. Due to the high concentration of C radicals on the outer surface of PS microspheres, the electrostatic adsorption effect promoted the enrichment of ethyl silicate (Fig. 2 a). Finally, a nano-level SiO2 shell was formed. We designed three PS cores with different particle sizes, I-III, and also designed two PS cores with the same particle size, II and IV. This would help us analyze the relationship between the core-shell structure of microspheres in I-IV PS-SiO2 PC and the FWHM of reflection spectra.

Firstly, PS microspheres with varying particle sizes (93–279 nm) were obtained by adjusting the concentrations of SDS and rotation speeds, as indicated in Table S1. The PDI of PS microspheres was approximately 0.005, indicating excellent monodispersity. Then, using these microspheres, different PS-SiO2 microspheres with inner diameters (PS) and thicknesses of shell layers (SiO2) were successfully prepared. PS-SiO2 core-shell structured nano-particles were achieved by different concentrations of NH3·H2O (1–9 mL) or TEOVS (0.75–2.1 mL) (Figs. S3a and c). The positive correlation coefficients (R2) between λ max and the amount of NH3·H2O and TEOVS added were 96.7 % and 97 %, respectively. PC materials spanning the full visible light range could be prepared according to the λ max required by composites (Figs. S3b and d).

Therefore, we showed and discussed four representative PS-SiO2 microspheres (Fig. 2b and c). During the preparation process, only three types of PS microspheres were used. The reflection spectra of PS PCs prepared from these microspheres exhibited λ max at 426 nm, 461 nm, and 524 nm, corresponding to purple, blue and green, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. They displayed FWHM values of 35 nm, 35.1 nm, and 37 nm, respectively (Fig. S4). Using ImageJ tool to calculate particle size, the error ranges for PS inner diameter and SiO2 shell were <2 nm and <1 nm, respectively. Fig. 2e displayed the dimensions of the PS core and SiO2 shell layers of PS-SiO2 microspheres: (I) 165 nm and 27 nm, (II) 187 nm and 30 nm, (III) 216 nm and 33 nm, and (IV) 187 nm and 41 nm, respectively. As the particle size of PS microspheres increased, the thickness of SiO2 followed the order: III > II > I. Due to the increased hydrolysis of TEOVS, the thickness of SiO2 was greater for IV than for II. It could be inferred that two factors, the particle size of PS and the amount of TEOVS, both had a positive correlation with the particle formation process.

Utilizing consistent vertical deposition techniques ensured obtaining uniform lattice superposition and similar structural color reflection intensity across the PS-SiO2 PCs with FWHM values of (I) 55 nm, (II) 67 nm, (III) 69 nm, and (IV) 92 nm (Fig. 2f). The FWHM could be controlled by changing the ratio of the core-shell structure, without impacting the intensity of reflected light [51]. Furthermore, we calculated the SiO2 volume ratio of the microspheres and obtained the data distribution plot in Fig. S5. The PS-SiO2 PCs exhibited regular changes with the size of different core-shell microspheres. FWHM increased with the size of the PS-SiO2 core-shell increased, which could be explained by the Bragg diffraction process. With the increasing thickness of the core-shell structured microspheres, the phenomenon of secondary refraction became more pronounced (λ 1 +λ 2), resulting in an increased FWHM (Fig. S6). Moreover, with continuous increase in the SiO2 shell thickness, a broader wavelength range was reflected. Another group of PC samples with sequentially increasing shell thicknesses were shown in Fig. S7, and their FWHM increased sequentially at 50.8 nm, 54.2 nm, and 56.3 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of hollow SiO2/PEGDA-ETPTA
ce:section.ce:para: The vibrant structural color of PS-SiO2 with a core-shell structure originated from the relative refractive index (Δn) between the PS-SiO2 (∼1.51) and air (1). However, after the gel (1.46) was filled, Δn only amounted to 0.05. Therefore, high refractive index composite materials with periodic stacking of hollow SiO2 were prepared by calcining the PS core. Figs. S8 and 9 verified the impact of high-temperature calcination on the core-shell structure. The composite gel comprising the hollow photonic structure exhibited a higher brightness compared to the original core-shell structure. When ethanol was dropped onto two hollow SiO2 PCs with different particle sizes, the patterned "butterfly" area rapidly exhibited blue and green colors. Subsequently, as the ethanol evaporated, the color faded away (Movie S1).

In particular, we prepared PS-SiO2 microspheres with larger particle sizes to make different PC templates. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, the PS-SiO2 PCs with λ max of 759 nm and 663 nm, respectively, exhibited reflection intensities exceeding 60 %, while the average transmittance was only about 25 %. The low transmittance of the PCs was due to the broad reflection wavelength of the PCs. The blue-purple color in the optical photo of Fig. 3a was the signal generated by Bragg second-order diffraction of the PS-SiO2 PC (Fig. S10) [52]. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, the calcined hollow SiO2 PCs showed distinct green (545 nm) and purple (465 nm) colors, which were attributed to the removal of PS during the calcination process. The statistical process of structural color change was shown in Fig. 3e, with a blue shift of approximately 200 nm observed for λ max, and the intensity of the structural color decreased by almost two-third. According to the decreasing law of refractive index in Bragg's law, the reflection intensity of hollow SiO2 PCs were less than 25 %. The FWHM of hollow SiO2 PCs were only 36 nm (Fig. S11). A comparison of the micrographs before calcination (Fig. 3f) and after calcination (Fig. 3g) revealed that the crystal FCC (111) lattice structure remained unchanged, confirming the calcination preparation process after self-assembly did not affect the lattice arrangement. The results were shown in Fig. S12. Here, Figs. S12a, b, c, and d represented PS-SiO2 PCs with particle sizes of 237, 264, 306, and 303 nm, respectively, while Figs. S12e, f, g, and h represented hollow SiO2 PCs with particle sizes of 225, 244, 256, and 248 nm (Fig. S12i-p). Notably, after high-temperature calcination, the particle size of PS-SiO2 microspheres contracted to a certain extent [53].

Traditional hydrogels, when stored at room temperature, were prone to water absorption or drying, leading to curling, shrinkage, and fracture, which inevitably cause irreparable damage to the materials [54–56]. To address this issue, we prepared a semi hydrophilic gel, with a sufficient Young's modulus and elastic deformation to resist swelling and cracking. By adjusting the composition of PEGDA (700) and the cross-linking curing monomer ETPTA, the hollow SiO2 PCs template was filled and solidified, recording as hollow SiO2/PEGDA-ETPTA. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the disappearance of the characteristic absorption peak of the C=C double bond in the wavelength range of 1634 cm−1 from the FT-IR spectrum indicated the completion of the photopolymerization reaction of the gel. Under normal conditions, the optical properties of the PEGDA-ETPTA gel exhibited over 90 % high transparency, with no significant difference in transparency compared to pure PEGDA and pure ETPTA gels (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, this experiment investigated the distinct curing effects associated with the use of two different monomers, namely PEGDA and PEGMA. Fig. S13 showed that when the monomer-to-cross-linking agent ratio was set at 2:1 and immersion was carried out for 3 h, both PEGDA-ETPTA and PEGMA-ETPTA exhibited a relatively complete gel state. As the quantity of PEGDA or PEGMA increased, gels with varying ratios (e.g., 4:1, 6:1) exhibited pronounced water absorption, with PEGMA displaying more distinct deformation and fragmentation (Table S2). PEGDA, ETPTA, PEGDA-ETPTA = 2:1 and PEGDA-ETPTA = 8:7 gel were selected for testing. As shown in Fig. S14, the interface water contact angles of ETPTA, PEGDA-ETPTA = 8:7, PEGDA-ETPTA = 2:1, and PEGDA were obtained to be 52.6°, 37.5°, 35.5°, and 26.5°, respectively. The hydrophilicity order based on contact angles was: PEGDA >2:1 > 8:7 > ETPTA, indicating their hydrophilic nature. However, with an increase in ETPTA content, the gel's crosslinking degree rose, leading to larger contact angles and reduced hydrophilicity. The same results were observed in Fig. 4c and d, demonstrating a clear correlation between the mechanical properties and water permeability of the gels with different relative content of PEGDA and ETPTA. Correspondingly, as the amount of PEGDA increased, both the fracture tensile strength and toughness of the gel continuously decreased, while the water permeability increased. On the contrary, when the amount of ETPTA reached its maximum, the gel exhibited fracture strength of 100 MPa, but nearly zero water permeability. After soaking in water for 3h, the mechanical properties of the gel decreased to a certain extent, but the amplitude reduction ratio of PEGDA-ETPTA = 8:7 was weaker than PEGDA-ETPTA = 2:1 (Fig. S15), indicating that increasing the content of ETPTA continuously optimized the mechanical properties of pure PEGDA after wet water. Therefore, the appropriate ratio of monomers to crosslinking agents ensured the suitability of the material in high humidity or water immersion environments. Finally, from the surface structure (Fig. 4e) and cross section (Fig. 4h) of hollow SiO2/PEGDA-ETPTA, it could be seen that the gaps of nano-structured microspheres were completely filled.

The refractive index of PEGDA-ETPTA was 1.46, significant structural color changes were observed when it was filled into hollow SiO2. As shown in Fig. 4f and g, hollow SiO2/PEGDA-ETPTA samples with reflection peaks at 605 nm and 526 nm were successfully prepared, with reflection intensities of 57 % and 48 %, and average transmittances of 26 % and 21 %, respectively. Compared with hollow SiO2 PCs, it could be concluded that the brightness of PC structural colors depended on the change in Δn of the composite material. Since the Δn of PS-SiO2 PC was 0.51, resulting in the highest reflection intensity, while for hollow SiO2 PC, it was the lowest (Δn = 0.15). The Δn of hollow SiO2/PEGDA-ETPTA was 0.46, resulting in intermediate reflection intensity.

Hollow SiO2/PEGDA-ETPTA PCs exhibited a red shift in structural coloration compared to hollow SiO2 PCs (Fig. 4i). This was because the presence of the gel replaced the air in the gap between the hollow SiO2 spheres, leading to the migration of structural color. Based on the Bragg diffraction law (Formula 1) and the average refractive index (Formula 2), the following analysis was conducted:

Visual sensing of hollow SiO2/PEGDA-ETPTA presented additional advantages in practical applications. Herein, the angle change behavior of two representative orange and green hollow SiO2 PCs/PEGDA-ETPTA was displayed. By testing the diffuse reflection spectra from 0–60°, the blue shifts of 625 nm–547 nm and 515 nm–445 nm were obtained, respectively (Fig. 4j and k). It could be seen that the intensity of structural color gradually decay to less than 20 % of the original color. The relationship curves revealed that the angle change trend revealed a weak linear relationship with the λ max with the angle change 70–78 nm. Such an angle-dependent structural color, easily distinguishable to the naked eye, had potential applications in enhancing security through anti-counterfeiting security [32,57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence regulation process of hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs fluorophores offered the advantages of small volume and large specific surface area, making them beneficial additives dispersed in pre-polymer solution. In quantum mechanics, according to Fermi's golden rule [58], the modulation effect of PBG on PL depends on the position of the fluorescent group [59,60]. Therefore, we systematically investigated the dynamic regulation of structural color and fluorescence at different positions. Herein, the results confirmed the successful formation of a stable GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA and PC framework, composed of 24 layers of microspheres with an approximate thickness of 5 μm (Fig. S16).

GQDs was fixed in the gel network through dipole-dipole interactions, and no overflow occurred after multiple water immersion experiments (Fig. S17). Observing the morphology, size, and position distribution of GQDs under TEM, three obvious GQDs distribution regions were shown in Fig. 5 a, each ranging from 10 to 12 nm. By measuring the size of GQDs within these regions, diameters of 1–5 nm were obtained. These GQDs with different particle sizes emitted light of various colors, forming a white like emission [61,62]. GQDs were tested by fluorescence spectroscopy and UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 5b. Herein, the λ max of GQDs fluorescence was 489 nm, which was originally a blue light emission. However, the FWHM of the emission peak was actually 157 nm, and the λ 2 reached 592 nm orange yellow light. This observation suggested that GQDs emitted combined light, capable of displaying white color after RGB color mixing.

Under a UV lamp, GQDs were extensively excited within a spatial range (Movie S2). Formula 3 defined the FWHM position as the wavelength on the left and right. The long wavelength was denoted as λ 2, and the short wavelength was denoted as λ 1. The range of fluorescence regulation was determined as the FWHM coverage area of green PC and orange PC, which were calculated as 33.33 % and 17.15 %, respectively (Fig. 5c). By utilizing the integration method, as shown in Formula 4, the product of the intensity and wavelength of the fluorescence curve represented the total signal quantity (S). When defining the GQDs fluorescence signal as S 0 (Formula 5), the FWHM positions of the green PC and orange PC were integrated, and their respective values were calculated as the theoretical values of fluorescence change(η).

(5)
S / S 0 = η

In addition, the end near the PC was designated as the ‘back’, and the end away from the PC was designated as the ‘front’. Fluorescence testing on the front (Fig. 5d) and back (Fig. 5e) was conducted using GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA, GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA + green PC, and GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA + orange PC. The fluorescence intensity increased towards the front and decreased towards the back. During the calculation of enhancement coefficients, we obtained the following results: for the front side, the green PC showed an enhancement of 77.4 %, and the orange PC showed an enhancement of 55.6 %. Correspondingly, there were 25 % and 42.2 % fluorescence intensities decay on the back, and the corresponding values of enhancement and attenuation indicated that, within a certain allowable range of error, it conformed to the law of energy conservation.

Due to the reversible conversion between the stable existence and attenuation of PBGs during water seeping into hollow SiO2 PCs, they had the potential to reversibly regulate the PL of fluorescent groups. Orange PCs were placed below the composite material, and PEGDA-ETPTA was set to a slow penetration state of 8:7. Fluorescence enhancement of GQDs was observed, gradually reaching its maximum level after 1.5 h (Fig. 5f). The contrast of blank gel was shown in Fig. S18, exhibiting a similar growth trend. When the concentration of GQDs was 1 mg/mL, the GQDs exhibited a self-quenching phenomenon [63,64]. The decrease in GQDs concentration caused by water immersion weakened the effect, resulting in an increase in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence spectra exhibited two prominent peaks, which experienced enhancement on both sides. This was a spectral coupling effect. Furthermore, in order to elucidate the fluorescence dynamic regulation effect of hollow SiO2 PCs, we utilized hollow SiO2 PCs of purple, blue, cyan, green, yellow, orange, and red colors to measure the fluorescence intensity values (Fig. 5g). Due to the significant overlap needed between the PL spectra and PBG, the green hollow SiO2 PC exhibited a more significant PL regulatory effect than the orange hollow SiO2 PC.

On the other hand, immersion of hollow SiO2 PCs in water resulted in a weakening of their color. The smaller water molecules were allowed to penetrate the non dense nanoscale pores of SiO2 and diffuse into the hollow sphere. After drying, they could return to their original color (Movie S3). To ensure that, a PEGDA:ETPTA ratio of 2:1 for GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA + Green PC was soaked in water for 3 days. The slight Δn ≈ 0.13 between the gel and water led to a weak structural color. The fluorescence decrease effect of GQDs after complete immersion was depicted in Fig. 5h, with 47 % lower than that after drying, consistent with the trend of weakened structural color following wetting (Fig. S19). Thus confirming that the reflection intensity (I max) of the hollow SiO2 PCs could impact the fluorescence intensity. The cyclic drying and wetting results were recorded in Fig. 5i, indicating the reversibility of the process. Therefore, the fluorescence regulation through adjusting the FWHM and λ max of hollow SiO2 PCs had been confirmed to be effective and feasible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical anti-counterfeiting applications and multidimensional encryption process of hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA labels
ce:section.ce:para: Using black patterns to spray ink on PVC to prepare a hollow photomask plate (Fig. 6 a), the 526 nm green PCs was combined with GQDs gel and cured under the photomask plate to obtain the GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA+ green PC "animal" tag. For instance, the “swan”, “deer” substrate of hollow SiO2 PCs/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA had a bright green PCs structural color. Under the irradiation of a UV lamp, the patterned area emitted blue white fluorescence (Fig. 6b).

Multiple "diamond" mixed color labels were prepared using the same experimental method as Fig. 6c, marked as: PEGDA-ETPTA, GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA, PEGDA-ETPTA+ green PC, GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA+ green PC, PEGDA-ETPTA+ orange PC and GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA+ orange PC, respectively. The color effect of the combination of structural colors and fluorescence under ultraviolet and sunlight provided rich recognition information, making it conducive to the application of practical anti-counterfeiting labels.

In order to better display the authentication process of this kind of material, we combined two sets of Arabic numerals "8" to obtain an identification label with a similar digital screen pattern. As shown in Fig. 6d, three types of diamond shaped modules which included PEGDA-ETPTA, GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA and GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA+ orange PC exhibited structural color, fluorescence, and fluorescence regulation with structural colors under sunlight or 365 nm light, respectively. The CIE distribution diagram for fluorescence testing was shown in Fig. S20. Mixed tones had regular shifts in color gamut distribution. The authentication mode of this type of label could be divided into two types: variable angle and ultraviolet irradiation. In the dark environment, the structural color label had only dark blurred pattern, showing "88" (Fig. S21a). After the lamp was turned on, the structural color was shown as a digital mode of orange and green. However, the identification was confused, which was read as orange "01", or "-E" (Fig. S21b). The corresponding unique output was unknown, but as the observation angle became larger, we obtained the final "01" signal (Fig. 6e). The second authentication mode was shown in Fig. 6f. Under the UV lamp condition, the structural color information was completely covered, showing the “smiling face” pattern (Fig. 6g). The patterns displayed different brightness on the front and back.

We further dug the information storage capacity and encoding capacity of the tags. When the orange structural color was coded as "1", the green structural color was coded as "0", and the excitation mode were defined as the possibility of two modes of fluorescence or no fluorescence, we obtained the corresponding maximum coding capability E c based on the array "8", "88" and "888" (Fig. 6h). In addition, assuming the number of types of quantum dots was A, seven structural colors and "k" fluorescent colors taken from "A" were selected to form "8" label. The encoding ability of the obtained anti-counterfeiting label reached
E c

(Fig. 6i). Moreover, we could further make various labels with binary numerical symbols, such as "01101". Therefore, the joint regulation of structural color and GQDs color has ultra-high digital information coding ability. Only by knowing the specific color arrangement and the specific fluorescence logo could we crack the encryption method, which was a potential encryption and anti-counterfeiting method.

Benefiting from the fluorescence regulation mechanism of hollow SiO2 PCs, we designed a highly secure intelligent verification process. Utilizing the rapid gel solidification ability, we assembled multiple 7 mm × 7 mm rectangular hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA modules through joining methods to obtain a 21 mm × 21 mm matrix label. By changing the viewing angle and illuminating with ultraviolet light, the matrix label displayed patterns with various color arrangements.

Combined with mobile recognition technology, matrices with structural and fluorescent colors under different conditions were prepared as anti-counterfeiting labels and applied to information encryption and decryption. First, we customized a set of coding terms (Fig. 7 a). The six structural colors presented in the color matrix squares, namely purple, blue, green, yellow, orange, and red, were defined as Arabic numerals "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", respectively. The different brightness levels of "blue-white" fluorescent color squares presented in the fluorescent matrix were defined as Arabic numerals "1" and "2". Then, according to the coding items, the color matrix and the fluorescent matrix formed by the anti-counterfeiting labels were converted into a digital matrix (Fig. 7b). By changing different angles, more structural color information could be obtained, and by changing the excitation direction, opposite fluorescence intensity information could be obtained. Finally, the digital matrix was compiled into encryption information and stored in the mobile application.

As a conceptual example, under the condition of θ = 0°and visible light irradiation, the anti-counterfeiting label presented color matrix A, which could be converted into a digital

. Then, the information receiver performed stepwise recognition and decryption based on the identification sequence created by the encryptor (e.g., A→B→C→D) (Fig. 7c). We further developed a simple matrix sequential recognition program to better demonstrate the process of stepwise recognition and decryption (Movie S4). Therefore, in order to obtain the correct information, the information receiver had to convert the four types of digital matrices mentioned and use a specific mobile application for stepwise recognition and decryption. If the digital matrix or identification sequence was incorrect, the correct information could not be obtained (Movie S5).

Herein, the security settings were divided into three dimensions: (1) the encoding key for color-to-digital matrix conversion, (2) the four recognition modes: sunlight front, angle change, fluorescence front and back, and (3) the verification order. If the used label was counterfeit or the recognition order are incorrect, the validation process would display as failed. This coding method could also be understood as a design process with high coding capacity. Combined with the intelligent authentication modes of angle variation and fluorescence positive and negative sides, the coding capacity of the encryption and decryption system could be up to (96)4 = 924. In addition, we could increase the array dimension to improve the encoding capability of anti-counterfeiting systems, such as designing an n × n anti-counterfeiting system. Through mathematical permutation and combination calculations, the coding capacity of the aforementioned system was (n × n)24. Therefore, anti-counterfeiting labels could realize step-by-step encryption and decryption modes for various information, which held significant value in the field of information security.

For many expensive foods and high-value goods with large daily circulation in the market, certification could be preliminarily defined based on the commodity attributes, applicable to merchants or individual consumers (Fig. 8 a). As a rapid identification tag, hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA labels could be designed according to specific application needs. For example, production and service enterprises often seek ISO quality certification, an internationally accepted means of quality management and trade facilitation tools (Fig. 8b). The ISO9000 standard defines the quality management and quality assurance system. ISO9001 and ISO9002, for example, can be represented by certified labels as "01" or "02" codes, respectively. Among them, ISO9001 is the quality assurance mode of product design, development, production, installation and service, which is applicable to enterprises with the whole production process including design, production, installation and service. ISO9002 only guarantees the quality of production, installation and service, applicable to enterprises with production, installation and service without design procedures. ISO9002 lacks all quality requirement systems for design control. Compared with ISO9002, ISO9001 is more stringent and the standard system is also the most complete standard system under the ISO9000 series, which represents the highest international quality certification standard.

In this regard, both merchants and consumers can safely verify the production type of the enterprise according to the sales label, requiring only observation of "01" or "02" to distinguish different grades of enterprise categories. In addition, using color matrix labels with numerical encoding can provide a high-level product quality certification process for the company. The matrix labels initially matched with the products are transported and sold to the secondary distributors or consumers. In order to ensure the authenticity of the trading products, they identified anti-counterfeiting labels through a verification mini program on smartphones under four conditions: white light irradiation, variable angle, UV lamp irradiation from front and back. After comparing the authentication key based on system input, the authenticity of the product is determined. This powerful protection function could effectively prevent counterfeit and inferior goods from circulating in the market, and thus to provide stronger support for social stability and economic environment security.

In summary, we developed a unified composite platform that integrates PCs and GQDs, which we efficiently encrypted and decrypted intricate information and achieve fluorescence regulation using this platform. Firstly, we described the methods for controlling the core-shell size of PS-SiO2 particles. Next, hollow SiO2 particles were obtained by calcining PS-SiO2 PCs at high temperature. Finally, we obtained various hollow SiO2/GQDs@PEGDA-ETPTA structures through the process of filling with prepolymer solution and UV curing. The synergistic effect of structural color and fluorescence was studied by preparing composite materials with different structural colors. The wide PBG PC structure significantly enhanced the effect of structural color controlled fluorescence. By synchronously adjusting the synergistic effect of structural color and fluorescence, both fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence suppression had been achieved, making great potential for the further application of composite materials in optical anti-counterfeiting. The distinct capability for fluorescence regulation not only facilitated identification but also enhanced the security against counterfeit identification labels. Our convenient UV curing strategy allowed us to produce diverse labels, including designs like "swan," "deer," a digital encoding of "88," and 3 × 3 digital matrix. Moreover, anti-counterfeiting labels could realize step-by-step encryption and decryption modes for providing correct information by mobile phones. The label could quickly detect and verify hidden information when exposed to four stimuli: white light exposure, angle change, UV light exposure, and flipping. Thus, this approach provides an effective strategy for efficiently identifying genuine products and holds considerable potential for anti-counterfeiting applications.
